turns.content: I'm reading a talk about the broadcast-skip pattern and I think it's just using tokio::sync::broadcast to send updates while workers skip messages they don't care about.

That's closer than you might think, but actually the core mechanism relies on a watch channel rather than a simple broadcast stream. In Rust this usually involves an Arc<tokio::sync::mpsc::Receiver> or similar where one thread subscribes to specific topics and another collects them into a skip queue. The real power comes from using a leader-follower epoch system to manage which messages are actually relevant.
So I can just use tokio::sync::broadcast with a receiver and have my followers iterate over it?

You cannot simply iterate over a standard broadcast receiver like that because it lacks the epoch tracking needed for proper skipping. The leader-follower pattern requires explicit coordination where the leader maintains an Arc<tokio::sync::watch::Receiver> and updates its state, while followers observe changes via tokio::sync::watch to detect when they should skip or process messages.
Okay, so I need to manage the epoch myself? Is there a crate that handles this?

There isn't a single crate that does everything perfectly out of the box yet, but you can implement it using tokio::sync::watch combined with an Arc<Mutex<skip_queue>>. The leader thread updates its epoch and pushes messages to the skip queue via tokio::sync::mpsc::Sender, while followers use tokio::sync::watch to monitor state changes and decide whether to pull from their local buffer or skip entirely based on the current epoch.
Wait, so I'm mixing multiple channels? Is that a problem with async Rust?

No, using tokio::sync::mpsc alongside tokio::sync::watch is actually idiomatic in high-performance systems. The key is ensuring the leader thread properly synchronizes its epoch via Arc<tokio::sync::watch> updates so followers can accurately determine whether to skip messages or process them based on their current state.
Got it, so I should avoid using plain tokio::sync::broadcast and instead use the watch pattern with proper epoch management?

Exactly. The broadcast-skip pattern specifically leverages the leader-follower relationship to coordinate message flow, ensuring that followers only process messages relevant to their current epoch while skipping others efficiently without blocking.

I'm trying to implement a leader-follower epoch system for my distributed log service, and I've got the basic replication working with the broadcast channel. But I noticed that when a follower skips a few messages during high load, it ends up reprocessing everything from the start instead of just catching up on the gap. Is there a better way to handle this skip scenario without rebuilding the entire state?

You're hitting exactly the right pain point with the broadcast-skip pattern. The issue is that your follower isn't tracking which specific messages it missed, so when you try to re-sync, it has to replay from epoch zero or the last checkpoint. Instead of using a simple watch channel for every message, you should implement an epoch-based skip mechanism where the leader maintains a gap list and sends only those skipped indices via the broadcast trait on the log store. This way, the follower can use a direct send to apply just the missing entries without iterating through everything.

That sounds good in theory, but I'm worried about the overhead of maintaining that gap list and sending extra broadcasts during every skip. My current implementation with the simple watch channel is actually faster than what you're describing because it avoids all that extra metadata traffic. Why would adding complexity ever be worth it when my basic approach works?

I get why your basic approach feels efficient right now, but the real bottleneck appears under sustained load where messages arrive in bursts. When you skip multiple entries, reprocessing from scratch causes the follower's worker threads to queue up again while your leader is still broadcasting new logs. The gap list strategy actually reduces the total number of broadcasts by batching skips into a single epoch update instead of triggering a full replay cycle every time a message is missed. It trades that metadata overhead for significantly lower latency during those high-load skip periods, which matters more when you're scaling to thousands of concurrent followers.

I'm trying to implement a leader-follower pattern in my Raft-like system using tokio, but I think the follower just needs to keep a simple counter and ignore messages it hasn't processed yet. Can't we just skip any broadcast that arrives after the last one?

That's a common misunderstanding of how the Broadcast-Skip pattern actually works in distributed systems like TiDB. You cannot simply ignore messages because your counter logic; you must use a watch channel to track the leader's epoch and ensure every broadcast is processed in order, even if it arrives later. The skip mechanism relies on the leader sending an explicit skip message with the current epoch number so followers know exactly which broadcasts were missed.

Okay, so I need to listen to the watch channel specifically for that skip message? Does the follower just register a subscription on the broadcast stream and then filter out anything that doesn't match the epoch?

Not exactly. The follower shouldn't be filtering; instead, it should receive all broadcasts from the leader's channel but maintain its own local state to track which epochs have been processed. When a skip message arrives with an epoch number greater than what the follower has seen, it updates its internal counter and continues processing subsequent broadcasts, ensuring no data is lost due to timing gaps.
